title: What Does a Police Station Representative Do? | PoliceStationRepUK

headings.text: What Does a Police Station Representative Do?
The Difference Between a Solicitor and a Representative
Why Firms Use Police Station Representatives

content:
Police station representatives play a vital role in the criminal justice system in England and Wales. They attend custody suites on behalf of criminal defence solicitors to advise and represent people who have been detained or asked to attend for interview. But what exactly does the role involve, and how does it fit within the wider legal framework?

The Role Explained

A police station representative (sometimes called a police station agent or rep) is an accredited professional who provides legal advice and assistance to suspects at police stations. They work under the supervision of a qualified solicitor and are authorised to attend custody suites, take instructions from detained persons, advise on the evidence and the appropriate response to police questions, sit in on police interviews, and make representations on bail and continued detention.

Representatives are not solicitors, but they are trained to the same standard for police station work. They hold accreditation through the Police Station Representatives Accreditation Scheme (PSRAS) administered by the Law Society, which confirms they have the knowledge and competence to advise at the police station.

How Representatives Are Accredited

To become an accredited police station representative, a person must complete a training programme covering criminal law, evidence, procedure, and the practical skills needed for custody work. They must pass the Police Station Qualification (PSQ) written examination, complete a portfolio of supervised police station attendances demonstrating competence across a range of offence types, and pass the Critical Incidents Test (CIT) — a practical assessment of their ability to handle difficult situations at the police station.

Once accredited, representatives are registered with the Solicitors Regulation Authority and can be deployed by any SRA-regulated firm to attend police stations on its behalf. They must complete continuing professional development to maintain their accreditation.

A Typical Police Station Attendance

When a solicitor instructs a representative to attend a police station, the process typically follows these steps. The representative receives the instruction (often via the DSCC or direct contact from the firm) and travels to the relevant custody suite. On arrival, they identify themselves to the custody sergeant, review the custody record, and obtain initial disclosure from the investigating officer. They then have a private consultation with the detained person to explain the allegation, discuss the evidence, and agree an interview strategy. During the police interview, the representative sits alongside the client, ensuring questions are fair, challenging any breaches of PACE, and intervening where necessary. After the interview, they make representations on charge, bail, or release. Finally, they prepare a detailed attendance note for the instructing solicitor.

What Offences Do Representatives Cover?

Police station representatives deal with the full range of criminal offences. This includes summary offences such as common assault, criminal damage, and public order matters; either-way offences including theft, fraud, ABH, and drug possession; and indictable-only offences such as robbery, GBH with intent, sexual offences, drug supply, and serious fraud. Experienced representatives are competent to handle even the most complex and serious matters at the police station stage.

The Difference Between a Solicitor and a Representative

The key distinction is qualification and scope of practice. A solicitor is a fully qualified legal professional who can conduct all types of legal work. A police station representative is specifically accredited for police station attendance and works under the supervision of a solicitor. At the police station itself, the practical role is very similar — both advise the client, attend interviews, and make representations. The representative cannot, however, appear in court or conduct other legal work outside the police station context without separate qualification.

Why Firms Use Police Station Representatives

Criminal defence firms use police station representatives for several practical reasons. Custody work is unpredictable and time-intensive — a single attendance can last several hours, often at unsocial times. By instructing a representative, the solicitor can maintain 24/7 duty solicitor coverage without attending every call-out personally, focus their own time on court work, conferences, and case preparation, meet Legal Aid Agency contract obligations for prompt attendance, and provide consistent quality of advice to clients in custody across multiple police stations simultaneously. Representatives are an essential part of the criminal defence infrastructure, and the profession depends on their availability and competence.
